ideas.title: Particle-vortex duality as blueprint for M5 brane dual computation
ideas.description: This idea draws a structural analogy between electromagnetic duality in M-theory and particle-vortex duality in condensed matter physics. In the fractional quantum Hall effect, direct computation with electrons at fractional filling is a strongly-coupled many-body problem. Jain's composite fermion approach performs a duality: electrons bind to an even number of flux quanta (vortices), producing composite fermions that see a reduced effective magnetic field and fill integer Landau levels. The strongly-coupled electron problem becomes a weakly-coupled composite fermion problem. Analogously, computing magnetic gravitational couplings using M2 branes — the electrically charged objects of M-theory — encounters strong-coupling difficulties. By passing to the dual M5 brane description, where M5 branes wrap internal cycles of the compactification manifold, the magnetic couplings become the natural (weakly-coupled) observables. The wrapping modes of M5 branes on calibrated cycles serve the same organizational role as flux attachment in composite fermion theory: they repackage the degrees of freedom so that the quantity of interest (magnetic gravitational coupling or Hall conductance) is computed at leading order rather than extracted from a non-perturbative resummation. Concretely, one should construct a duality map taking the M2 brane partition function to a sum over M5 wrapping sectors, identify the analog of the Jain projection that enforces correct flux attachment (here, the self-dual three-form constraint on the M5 worldvolume), and extract the gravitational couplings from the dual partition function where they appear as semiclassical saddle-point contributions.

ideas.title: Finite-size corrections to asymptotic degeneracy formulas: Lessons from statistical mechanics partition function exactification
ideas.description: The OSV conjecture relates black hole entropy to the topological string partition function in the large-charge asymptotic limit, but whether this relation is exact at finite charges remains an open question. We draw an analogy to statistical mechanics, where thermodynamic quantities derived in the infinite-system-size limit receive well-understood finite-size corrections. In statistical mechanics, the exact partition function at finite system size N differs from its thermodynamic limit by corrections computable via finite-size scaling theory, the Euler-Maclaurin formula, and the distribution of Lee-Yang zeros in the complexified fugacity plane. The key insight is that the asymptotic (thermodynamic limit) result is not fundamentally wrong but incomplete — it misses exponentially suppressed contributions that become important at finite N and can be systematically restored using resurgent asymptotics and trans-series. We propose adapting this framework to the OSV relation by: (1) treating the black hole charges as the analogue of system size and the mixed partition function as a finite-N statistical mechanical partition function; (2) applying resurgent trans-series techniques to identify non-perturbative sectors (instantons, D-brane contributions) that correct the large-charge OSV formula; (3) studying the analytic structure of the black hole partition function in complexified chemical potential space to locate analogues of Lee-Yang zeros, whose finite-charge distribution would reveal whether the OSV relation holds exactly up to computable non-perturbative corrections or requires genuine structural modification at finite charges. This approach reframes the problem from asking 'is OSV exact?' to 'what is the complete trans-series completion of OSV?', providing a systematic path to resolving the finite-charge question.

ideas.title: Singular perturbation methods for the OSV conjecture in the small black hole regime
ideas.description: The OSV conjecture relates the mixed black hole partition function to the square of the topological string partition function, but its validity for small black holes—where higher-derivative F-terms in the prepotential dominate over the classical cubic term—remains unestablished. This situation is structurally analogous to the boundary layer problem in fluid dynamics: at high Reynolds number, the Navier-Stokes equations reduce to the Euler equations in the bulk, but near solid boundaries a thin layer exists where the highest-derivative (viscous) terms become leading order. Dropping them leads to loss of the no-slip boundary condition and an ill-posed problem, just as naively truncating higher-derivative corrections for small black holes destroys the entropy matching. Prandtl resolved this via matched asymptotic expansions: an inner expansion valid in the viscous layer and an outer expansion valid in the inviscid bulk, connected by matching conditions in the overlap. Adapting this framework, one introduces a small parameter epsilon proportional to the ratio of charges to the string scale. For epsilon much less than one (small black holes), the higher-derivative F_g terms with g >= 2 dominate, and the saddle-point of the OSV integral is controlled by these corrections—this is the inner expansion. For epsilon much greater than one (large black holes), the classical prepotential F_0 dominates and the standard attractor mechanism applies—this is the outer expansion. The matching region at intermediate epsilon constrains the analytic structure of the topological string free energy and may yield new non-renormalization conditions on the F_g couplings. A uniformly valid composite expansion, constructed by additive matching, would provide an OSV partition function valid across the entire charge range, offering concrete predictions for small black hole entropy that can be checked against microscopic state counting in cases where dual CFT descriptions exist.

ideas.title: Symplectically covariant OSV relation at finite charges
ideas.description: This project constructs a fully symplectically covariant mixed ensemble for black hole partition functions and uses it to re-examine the OSV conjecture beyond the large-charge asymptotic regime. The standard OSV relation is formulated in an electric frame that breaks symplectic invariance, making it impossible to distinguish true finite-size corrections from frame-dependent artifacts; by treating electric and magnetic charges on equal footing one obtains a duality-invariant partition function whose finite-charge structure is unambiguous. Within this covariant framework we systematically classify corrections order by order in inverse charges, identifying which terms are physical and which vanish upon restoring symplectic symmetry. The result is a sharp test of whether the OSV relation can be promoted to an exact identity or necessarily requires modification, with concrete predictions checkable against microscopic state counts in N=2 compactifications with both electric and magnetic charges turned on.

ideas.title: MIMO channel capacity computation via spectral genus expansion adapted to topological strings
ideas.description: In wireless telecommunications, the problem of computing exact channel capacity for large MIMO antenna arrays is structurally parallel to computing all-genus topological string free energies: both reduce to evaluating spectral quantities of large random matrices beyond the leading planar (large N) limit. The MIMO community solved this by developing a mature toolkit — loop equations (Schwinger-Dyson equations for resolvents), Coulomb gas methods mapping eigenvalue distributions to electrostatic equilibrium problems, and topological recursion on the spectral curve — to systematically extract 1/N^2 corrections that encode finite-antenna-number effects on capacity and outage statistics. This research idea adapts that engineering-driven computational pipeline to the topological string context. Specifically, one treats the spectral curve of the relevant matrix model (e.g., the Dijkgraaf-Vafa matrix model for a given Calabi-Yau geometry) as the analog of the MIMO channel transfer function, then applies the same recursive genus-by-genus free-energy extraction that yields finite-size capacity corrections in telecommunications. The cross-pollination is bidirectional: the richer geometric structure of topological strings (integrality constraints from BPS state counts, holomorphic anomaly equations, modularity) provides new consistency checks and resummation strategies that could flow back to improve finite-size approximations in large-array wireless systems. This method transfer leverages the fact that both communities independently developed overlapping but non-identical techniques for the same underlying mathematical problem — the topological expansion of matrix integrals — and merging these toolkits can yield more efficient algorithms for computing higher-genus amplitudes than either field possesses alone.

ideas.title: Restoring electric-magnetic symmetry via doubled formalism inspired by chiral symmetry restoration in lattice QCD
ideas.description: The conventional mixed ensemble in black hole thermodynamics treats electric and magnetic charges asymmetrically: electric charges are controlled by chemical potentials while magnetic charges are held fixed, explicitly breaking the symplectic Sp(2n,Z) electromagnetic duality. This situation closely parallels the chiral symmetry breaking problem in lattice QCD, where naive discretization of the Dirac action treats left-handed and right-handed fermion modes differently, breaking the continuous chiral symmetry of the continuum theory. In lattice QCD, the solution came through constructing formulations (Ginsparg-Wilson, overlap, domain-wall fermions) that restore exact chiral symmetry by doubling degrees of freedom or introducing auxiliary dimensions, treating both chiralities democratically at the fundamental level. Adapting this strategy, we propose constructing a doubled thermodynamic ensemble that introduces conjugate potentials for both electric and magnetic charges simultaneously, yielding a manifestly Sp(2n,Z)-covariant partition function. Concretely, this means defining a generating functional over the full symplectic vector of electromagnetic potentials (phi_e, phi_m), analogous to how domain-wall fermions introduce a fifth dimension to symmetrize chirality. The resulting duality-covariant free energy would transform as a proper symplectic scalar, and its Legendre transforms would reproduce all standard ensembles as partial projections — just as the chirally symmetric lattice action reduces to the naive action in appropriate limits. This framework would resolve known anomalies in entropy matching across duality frames and provide a natural setting for studying phase transitions that respect the full duality group of the theory.

ideas.title: Beyond one-loop Schwinger via systematic correction hierarchies inspired by post-Hartree-Fock quantum chemistry
ideas.description: The one-loop Schwinger pair-production rate in string and M-theory occupies a role analogous to the Hartree-Fock approximation in quantum chemistry: it is the leading-order result that captures the dominant physics but may miss qualitatively important corrections. In quantum chemistry, the post-Hartree-Fock revolution demonstrated that systematic inclusion of electron correlation — through Møller-Plesset perturbation theory at successive orders (MP2, MP3, MP4), coupled-cluster theory (CCSD, CCSD(T)), and ultimately non-perturbative full configuration interaction — could dramatically revise predictions, sometimes qualitatively changing ground-state properties. Crucially, diagnostic tools like the T1 and D1 diagnostics were developed to assess when single-reference (one-loop-like) results are trustworthy. Adapting this framework to the M-theory Schwinger problem, one would: (1) systematically compute two-loop and three-loop string amplitudes in the relevant background field, organized by genus expansion, analogous to the MP2/MP3 perturbative corrections; (2) include non-perturbative M5-brane instanton contributions classified by wrapping number, analogous to how full CI captures non-perturbative correlation beyond any finite perturbative order; (3) develop a convergence diagnostic — a ratio of the two-loop to one-loop contribution magnitude — that flags parameter regimes (field strengths, compactification scales) where the one-loop approximation fails, just as T1 > 0.02 flags unreliable single-reference chemistry. This hierarchical approach would map out precisely where in M-theory parameter space the weak-coupling one-loop Schwinger result is reliable and where qualitatively new physics from multi-loop or brane corrections emerges, potentially revealing phase-transition-like thresholds analogous to the multi-reference character transitions seen in strongly correlated molecular systems.

ideas.title: Self-consistent resurgence diagnostics for genus expansions via large-order verification and exact benchmarks
ideas.description: This work develops a validated resurgence framework for divergent genus expansions by combining high-precision numerical computation of large-order coefficients via topological recursion with systematic consistency checks against transseries predictions. Instead of assuming Borel summability and completeness of the non-perturbative sector decomposition, the method uses the asymptotic growth of genus-g free energies to independently extract Stokes constants and verify alien derivative relations, flagging incomplete or spurious sectors. The framework is calibrated against matrix models and topological string backgrounds where exact non-perturbative answers are independently known (double-scaled models, Painlevé equations, ABJM theory), establishing error bounds on extracted non-perturbative data. This adversarial design transforms resurgent analysis from an assumed structural input into a testable, falsifiable methodology with built-in diagnostics for failure.

papers.title: Microscopic origin of the Bekenstein-Hawking entropy
papers.abstract: Abstract The Bekenstein-Hawking area-entropy relation S BH = A 4 is derived for a class of five-dimensional extremal black holes in string theory by counting the degeneracy of BPS solition bound states.
